content.problemStatement:
You are given NNN points (x1,y1),(x2,y2),…,(xN,yN)(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_N, y_N)(x1​,y1​),(x2​,y2​),…,(xN​,yN​).

You should answer QQQ queries. In each query, you are given a range [f,t][f, t][f,t]. Consider the points (xf,yf),(xf+1,yf+1),…,(xt,yt)(x_f, y_f), (x_{f+1}, y_{f+1}), \ldots, (x_t, y_t)(xf​,yf​),(xf+1​,yf+1​),…,(xt​,yt​); the answer to a query is the square of the maximum Euclidean distance between any two of these points.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer N, the number of points. Each of the next N lines contains two space-separated integers xi and yi, the coordinates of the ith point. The next line contains an integer Q, the number of queries. Each of the next Q lines contains two space-separated integers f and t, describing a query.
content.outputFormat: For each query, print a single line containing one integer — the squared maximum distance.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each query, we calculate the Euclidean distance between all pairs of points in the given range and return the square of the maximum distance.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a brute force approach by iterating over all pairs of points in the given range and calculating the Euclidean distance between them. We keep track of the maximum distance found so far and update it whenever we find a larger distance. Finally, we return the square of the maximum distance.
solution.timeComplexity: O(Q * (t - f + 1)^2) — because for each query, we are iterating over all pairs of points in the range [f, t] and calculating the Euclidean distance between them.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — because we are only using a constant amount of space to store the maximum distance and the coordinates of the points.
